critic_raw:
Counterfactual Generative Networks (CGN) use 'counterfactual reasoning' as a conditioning mechanism for the generator, but the counterfactual context is described as 'sampled from rich prior knowledge sources.' This is circular: if counterfactual contexts are drawn from a prior knowledge distribution rather than from a structural causal model, the 'counterfactual' conditioning is actually just multi-source conditional generation with a rebranded vocabulary. A genuine counterfactual requires specifying what would have been the case had a specific cause been different — this requires a causal model, not a prior distribution. The paper likely conflates causal counterfactuals with statistical conditioning on auxiliary inputs.

FID and IS are used as the primary GAN evaluation metrics. Both have well-documented limitations: IS measures sample quality and diversity relative to a single Inception v3 network trained on ImageNet, which makes it poorly calibrated for domains far from ImageNet statistics. FID measures distributional similarity between generated and real samples but is sensitive to sample size (typically requires at least 10k samples for stable estimation) and does not penalize mode coverage failure when modes are smooth. For counterfactual generation specifically — where the evaluation goal is whether generated counterfactuals are realistic AND causally meaningful — neither FID nor IS measures causal meaningfulness.

The discriminator in CGN is described as evaluating 'authenticity against counterfactual conditions.' This raises a fundamental training signal question: the discriminator must distinguish real samples from generated samples conditioned on a counterfactual context, but the training data contains no real samples paired with counterfactual contexts (counterfactuals by definition didn't happen). The discriminator therefore trains on an impoverished real-data signal and a generated counterfactual signal that may not be comparable, creating a training instability that the paper does not characterize.

Diversity and realism are claimed to improve, but diversity in GAN evaluation is typically measured by comparing the variance of generated samples against the variance of real samples. If the counterfactual conditioning introduces a new source of variation (different counterfactual contexts produce different images), diversity metrics will improve simply due to this additional conditioning variable — not because the model has learned a more diverse underlying distribution. The paper must control for this conditioning-induced diversity before claiming improved diversity as a model property.
defender_raw:
Counterfactual Generative Networks (CGN) address a well-documented failure mode of standard discriminative classifiers: shape-texture biases that lead to brittle predictions. By generating counterfactually augmented data — images where causal factors (shape, texture, background) can be independently varied — CGN provides training data that forces classifiers to rely on the correct causal features (shape) rather than spurious correlates (texture).

The GAN architecture conditioned on counterfactual queries is the right technical choice for this goal. The generator must produce images that are photorealistic (to be useful as training data) while satisfying counterfactual constraints (independent variation of causal factors). A GAN's adversarial training objective is well-suited to photorealism, and conditioning on counterfactual queries provides the structural constraint.

The conditioning on 'rich prior knowledge sources' — specifically, independent shape, texture, and background generators — reflects the causal model of image generation. The assumption that shape, texture, and background are causally independent (conditional on object category) is motivated by the way real-world objects are perceived and is the causal model underlying the texture-bias literature.

Using IS and FID as evaluation metrics is appropriate because the primary use case of the generated data is as training data for downstream classifiers. High-fidelity, diverse generated samples (captured by IS and FID) are necessary for the downstream classifiers to benefit from counterfactual augmentation.

An anticipated critique is that the causal independence assumption (shape, texture, background are independent) is violated for many natural image categories where texture and shape are correlated. The defense is that the goal is not to model the data-generating process faithfully but to generate augmented training data that breaks spurious correlations — even approximate causal independence in the generated data may be sufficient to reduce texture-bias in downstream classifiers.

Another anticipated critique is that IS and FID are imperfect proxies for the actual goal (downstream classifier robustness). The defense is that the paper also evaluates downstream classifier performance on shape/texture-biased test sets, providing a direct measure of the counterfactual generation's utility.
all_issues_raised: The 'counterfactual' conditioning is likely statistical conditioning from a prior distribution rather than conditioning from a structural causal model — the vocabulary of counterfactual reasoning is applied to what is technically multi-source conditional generation.

FID and IS are inadequate for evaluating counterfactual generation: IS is miscalibrated for non-ImageNet domains, FID does not penalize mode coverage failure, and neither metric captures causal meaningfulness of the generated counterfactuals.

The discriminator trains on real samples without counterfactual context paired against generated samples with counterfactual context — this asymmetric training signal has no real-data anchor in the counterfactual dimension, creating a training instability that is not characterized.

Improved diversity metrics may reflect additional variation from different counterfactual conditioning inputs rather than a more diverse underlying model — the paper must control for conditioning-induced diversity before attributing improvement to the model architecture.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 'counterfactual' conditioning is likely statistical conditioning from a prior distribution rather than conditioning from a structural causal model — the vocabulary of counterfactual reasoning is applied to what is technically multi-source conditional generation.

FID and IS are inadequate for evaluating counterfactual generation: IS is miscalibrated for non-ImageNet domains, FID does not penalize mode coverage failure, and neither metric captures causal meaningfulness of the generated counterfactuals.

The discriminator trains on real samples without counterfactual context paired against generated samples with counterfactual context — this asymmetric training signal has no real-data anchor in the counterfactual dimension, creating a training instability that is not characterized.

Improved diversity metrics may reflect additional variation from different counterfactual conditioning inputs rather than a more diverse underlying model — the paper must control for conditioning-induced diversity before attributing improvement to the model architecture.
